title: How to build routines that support regulation without becoming rigid
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The myth of the perfectly structured life

I spent years believing that if I could just build the right routine, I would finally feel okay. I would wake up at 5 AM, meditate for 20 minutes, journal for 15, exercise for 30, eat a perfect breakfast, and then glide into my day like some kind of regulated human being. Here's the thing. That fantasy never survived contact with reality.

My nervous system doesn't care about my plans. It doesn't care about the color-coded schedule I made in my bullet journal at 2 AM when I couldn't sleep because my body was stuck in hyperarousal. It cares about safety. It cares about predictability. It cares about rhythm. But it doesn't care about rigidity.

There's a difference between a routine that supports regulation and a routine that becomes another cage. I've built both. I've lived in both. And I can tell you that the second one feels like safety at first, then slowly turns into suffocation.

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed polyvagal theory, talks about how our nervous system is constantly scanning for cues of safety and danger. This happens below conscious awareness, all day every day. When we build routines that are too rigid, too demanding, too punishing, our nervous system reads that as a threat. It's like we're telling our body, "You must perform perfectly or something bad will happen." And the body responds accordingly. With tension. With vigilance. With eventual collapse.

So how do we build routines that actually support regulation without turning into rigid structures that our system rebels against? Let me walk you through what I've learned, mostly by failing repeatedly.

Start smaller than you think you need to

I mean smaller. Like, embarrassingly small.

When I first started trying to build regulation-friendly routines, I would design these elaborate morning protocols. Cold plunge. Breathwork. Yoga. Affirmations. Gratitude list. The whole thing. And I would sustain it for maybe three days before my nervous system would start screaming at me. Then I'd feel shame. Then I'd try harder. Then I'd fail harder. Then I'd give up entirely for weeks.

Sound familiar?

What finally worked was starting with one thing. Just one. I committed to placing my feet on the floor in the morning and taking three conscious breaths before I stood up. That's it. Three breaths. Took maybe 45 seconds. But I did it every day for two months. And something shifted.

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, talks about pendulation. The idea that healing happens in small, manageable oscillations between activation and settling. Not in grand, dramatic transformations. The nervous system learns through tiny, repeated experiences of safety. Not through massive overhauls.

So here's my question for you. What is the smallest possible version of a routine that might feel supportive? Can you drink one glass of water when you wake up? Can you step outside for 60 seconds? Can you put your hand on your chest and take one slow exhale? Stay with me here. That's not nothing. That's the foundation.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work on emotional flashbacks and inner critic work helped me understand why I was so harsh with myself when I couldn't maintain my rigid routines. The book doesn't prescribe a specific structure. Instead, it helps you understand the patterns that make structure feel threatening in the first place.

Build in flexibility from the start

Here's the thing about nervous systems that have experienced trauma. They don't trust rigidity. They've been controlled before. They've been forced into compliance. They've learned that rigid structures often come with punishment attached.

So when you build a routine that says "I must do this at exactly this time every day or I've failed," your system might interpret that as another form of control. Another demand to perform. Another opportunity for shame.

What if instead, you built routines with built-in options? What if you had a morning practice that could take 5 minutes or 30 minutes, depending on how your body felt? What if you had a "minimum viable" version and a "full" version, and both counted as success?

I call this the choose-your-own-adventure approach to regulation. And it works because it honors the reality that our capacity fluctuates. Some days I can do a full yoga practice. Some days I can barely stand up. Both days deserve a routine that meets me where I am.

Dan Siegel, the psychiatrist who developed interpersonal neurobiology, talks about the window of tolerance. That's the zone where we can think, feel, and be present without getting overwhelmed or shutting down. A good routine should keep you inside that window. Not push you out of it.

If your routine is too rigid, it will push you into hyperarousal (anxiety, panic, agitation) when you can't maintain it. Or it will push you into hypoarousal (numbness, collapse, dissociation) when it demands more than you have. A flexible routine, on the other hand, adapts to where your window of tolerance is today.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link). Nedra Glover Tawwab's work isn't directly about trauma, but the principles apply beautifully here. You need boundaries with your own routines. You need to be able to say no to the part of you that demands perfection. You need to protect your nervous system from your own inner taskmaster.

Let your body lead, not your mind

Most of us build routines from the neck up. We think about what we "should" do. We plan based on what looks good on paper. We design structures that make logical sense but feel terrible in our bodies.

I did this for years. I would create a schedule that looked perfect on my planner but left me feeling wired and exhausted by noon. My mind was happy. My body was screaming.

Pat Ogden, the founder of Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, emphasizes that the body holds the blueprint for healing. She talks about how we need to work with the body's wisdom, not override it. So when you're building a routine, start by asking your body what it needs. Not your inner critic. Not your productivity-obsessed mind. Your actual body.

What does that look like practically? It means pausing before you start your routine. Putting a hand on your belly. Taking a breath. Noticing what you actually feel. Do you need movement or rest? Do you need stimulation or quiet? Do you need connection or solitude?

The answer will change day to day. That's not a problem. That's information.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link). Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey's conversation-based book helps shift the question from "what's wrong with you" to "what happened to you." That same shift applies to our routines. Instead of asking "what's wrong with me that I can't stick to this schedule," ask "what happened that made this schedule feel unsafe for my system?"

Routines as returning, not as performing

Here's the reframe that changed everything for me. I stopped thinking of routines as something I had to perform correctly. I started thinking of them as ways to return to myself.

Returning. Not arriving. Not achieving. Returning.

Because here's the truth. I'm going to get dysregulated. You're going to get dysregulated. Life is going to happen. Triggers will come. The nervous system will get activated. That's not failure. That's being human.

Routines aren't about preventing dysregulation. They're about creating pathways back to regulation when we inevitably get knocked off balance. They're the familiar road home when the world feels chaotic and unsafe.

Think about it this way. When a child is scared, they don't need a complex protocol. They need a familiar voice. A familiar touch. A familiar rhythm. That's what routines can be for our nervous systems. A familiar rhythm that says "you're okay, you're here, you're safe."

But only if they're flexible enough to actually be there when we need them. A rigid routine that requires perfect execution isn't a comfort. It's another demand. Another way to fail.

So build your routines like you're building a home for a nervous system that has been through war. Build them sturdy but not rigid. Build them with multiple entrances and exits. Build them so that even on the worst days, there's a way in.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link). Judith Herman's work laid the groundwork for understanding how trauma disrupts our sense of safety and connection. She describes recovery as happening in stages, and that's how I think about routines too. Start with stabilization. Build safety first. Then expand from there.

The practice of letting go

I'll be honest with you. I still struggle with this. I still have days where I catch myself building rigid structures in my mind, telling myself that if I just follow this exact plan, I'll finally feel okay. And I still have to remind myself that the plan isn't the point. The regulation is the point.

Sometimes the most regulating thing I can do is abandon my entire routine and sit on the floor with my dog for ten minutes. Sometimes the most regulating thing is to eat something warm and simple. Sometimes it's to call a friend and say nothing important.

The routine is a container. Not a cage. It holds us, but it doesn't trap us. It supports us, but it doesn't define us.

And when we inevitably fall out of our routines, when we miss a day or a week or a month, the way back is not through shame. It's not through punishment. It's through gentle return. One small step. One conscious breath. One hand on the chest.

That's the practice. Not the perfect execution. The return.

So here's my invitation to you. Look at whatever routines you currently have or want to build. Ask yourself honestly: does this support my nervous system or control it? Does this help me feel safe or does it add pressure? Can I do a smaller version of this that still counts?

And if the answer to any of those questions makes you uncomfortable, good. That discomfort is information. That's your system telling you something important. Listen to it.

Your body has been trying to keep you safe for your entire life. Maybe it's time to let it help you build the routines that actually work.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) - A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors
  • What Happened to You? (paid link) - A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) - One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery
